hypotheses: European Americans will respond to mortality salience with self-protective strategies aimed at affirming the self and derogating dissimilar others.
Asian Americans will respond to mortality salience by affirming other people and becoming less derogating of those dissimilar to the self.
claim.hypotheses_location: Page 1020, left column, paragraph beginning 'We hypothesized that mortality salience should produce divergent cultural responses.'
claim.statement: Mortality salience promoted culturally divergent responses, leading European Americans to defend the self and Asian Americans to defend other people.

data.source: Laboratory experiments with university student participants
data.sample_size: Study 1: 22 European Americans + 20 Asian Americans = 42; Study 2: 48 European Americans + 23 Asian Americans = 71
data.notes: Participants completed standard TMT mortality-salience manipulation, PANAS delay task, and target-evaluation measures (PAS, bail amount, ABS).

method.description: Two laboratory experiments primed participants with either a mortality-salience manipulation (writing about their own death) or a control topic (dental pain), administered a delay task (PANAS), and then measured attitudes toward a culturally non-normative target (Study 1) or blame assigned to an innocent victim (Study 2). Analyses compared European American and Asian American participants across conditions.
steps: Recruit European American and Asian American undergraduate participants.
Randomly assign participants to mortality-salience or control (dental pain) writing task.
Administer PANAS as a filler/delay task.
Study 1: Provide fabricated legal brief about a prostitute; collect bail amount and Prostitution Attitudes Scale responses.
Study 2: Provide fabricated news article about an innocent victim; collect Assignment of Blame Scale responses.
Analyze with 2 (condition) × 2 (ethnicity) ANOVAs and planned contrasts; test mood covariate.
method.models: Multivariate ANOVA, univariate ANOVA, planned t-contrasts
method.outcome_variable: Study 1: Prostitution Attitude Score (PAS) and bail amount; Study 2: Assignment of Blame Score (ABS)
method.independent_variables: Mortality-salience condition (death vs dental pain) and participant ethnicity (European American vs Asian American)

results.summary: Across both experiments, mortality salience triggered opposite patterns in the two cultural groups: European Americans showed increased derogation or blame (more negative PAS scores, higher bail, greater victim blame), whereas Asian Americans showed decreased derogation or blame, effectively affirming others. These interactions were statistically significant and not explained by mood or gender.

numerical_results.outcome_name: Prostitution Attitude Score difference (European Americans: mortality vs control)
Assignment of Blame Score difference (European Americans: mortality vs control)

metadata.original_paper_title: Culturally Divergent Responses to Mortality Salience
